section.title: The Suffering Servant
section.sectionRationale: This is the summit of the Book of Comfort and arguably of the entire Old Testament's witness to Christ, the fourth and final Servant Song. Everything Isaiah has been building toward, the arm of the LORD bared, the good news proclaimed, the exile answered, gathers here into a single figure who is despised, pierced, crushed, silent before His accusers, and yet exalted above all. No Old Testament passage is quoted or echoed by the New Testament writers about Jesus more directly or more often.
section.thesis: The Servant of the LORD bears, in His own body and by God's own will, the guilt, punishment, and sickness that belonged to His people, dying an unjust death as a guilt offering for the many, and is afterward raised to see His offspring, prolong His days, and divide the spoils, because His substitutionary suffering has already succeeded.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the Servant exalted though disfigured; who has believed this report; a man of sorrows despised and stricken; He was pierced for our transgressions; and the aftermath, a grave with the rich, offspring, and intercession for the transgressors.

units.label: The Servant exalted though disfigured
units.note: kings will shut their mouths because of Him

verses.text: Behold, My {{ebed|Servant}} will prosper; He will be raised and lifted up and highly exalted.
Just as many were appalled at Him, His appearance was disfigured beyond that of any man, and His form was marred beyond human likeness,
so He will {{nazah|sprinkle}} many nations. Kings will shut their mouths because of Him. For they will see what they have not been told, and they will understand what they have not heard.

units.label: Who has believed our message
units.note: no beauty that we should desire Him

verses.text: Who has believed our message? And to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed?
He grew up before Him like a tender shoot, and like a root out of dry ground. He had no stately form or majesty to attract us, no beauty that we should desire Him.
He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows, acquainted with grief. Like one from whom men hide their faces, He was despised, and we esteemed Him not.

units.label: Pierced for our transgressions
units.note: the punishment that brought us peace

verses.text: Surely He took on our infirmities and carried our sorrows; yet we considered Him stricken by God, struck down and afflicted.
But He was pierced for our transgressions, He was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was upon Him, and by His stripes we are healed.
We all like sheep have gone astray, each one has turned to his own way; and the LORD has laid upon Him the {{avon|iniquity}} of us all.
He was oppressed and afflicted, yet He did not open His mouth. He was led like a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before her shearers is silent, so He did not open His mouth.
By oppression and judgment He was taken away, and who can recount His descendants? For He was cut off from the land of the living; He was stricken for the transgression of My people.

units.label: A guilt offering for the many

verses.text: He was assigned a grave with the wicked, and with a rich man in His death, although He had done no violence, nor was any deceit in His mouth.

Yet it was the LORD’s will to crush Him and to cause Him to suffer; and when His soul is made a {{asham|guilt offering}}, He will see His offspring, He will prolong His days, and the good pleasure of the LORD will prosper in His hand.
After the anguish of His soul, He will see the light of life and be satisfied. By His knowledge My righteous Servant will justify {{rabbim|many}}, and He will bear their iniquities.

units.label: Numbered with the transgressors
units.note: He poured out His life unto death

verses.text: Therefore I will allot Him a portion with the great, and He will divide the spoils with the strong, because He has poured out His life unto death, and He was numbered with the transgressors. Yet He bore the sin of many and made intercession for the transgressors.

ebed.gloss: Servant, one who belongs to and serves another. Across Isaiah 40 to 53 this title deepens from Israel's calling to a single figure who succeeds where Israel fails, culminating here in a Servant who is exalted precisely through humiliation and death, then raised and highly exalted, Philippians 2:8-9.

nazah.gloss: To sprinkle, the verb used for priestly sprinkling of blood in atonement rituals, Leviticus 4 and 16. The Servant will sprinkle many nations, language that casts His coming exaltation in priestly, cleansing terms, He purifies the nations by what He suffers.

avon.gloss: Iniquity, guilt, the twisted moral weight of sin and its consequence together. The LORD causes the avon of us all to strike, literally to meet or converge upon, the Servant, a picture of guilt being transferred rather than merely observed or forgiven in the abstract.

asham.gloss: Guilt offering, the specific Levitical sacrifice for offenses requiring restitution, Leviticus 5-7. The Servant's soul is made an asham, the only place in the Old Testament where a person, rather than an animal, becomes this sacrifice, marking His death as substitutionary atonement in the most technical sacrificial vocabulary Israel had.

rabbim.gloss: The many, a term used three times in this chapter, verses 11 and 12, for those whose iniquities the Servant bears and whom He justifies. It signals a vast, though not necessarily universal, scope of substitution, echoed by Jesus Himself, His life a ransom for many, Mark 10:45, and Paul's for many will be made righteous, Romans 5:19.

basic.context: This is the fourth and final Servant Song, and it stands at the theological center of the entire Old Testament. A figure appears who is despised, disfigured, silent before His accusers, and put to death, yet whose suffering is described as bearing the sins of others and satisfying God's own will, after which He is raised and exalted.
basic.hermeneutics: Jewish and Christian readers have long debated who this Servant is, Israel corporately, a righteous prophet, or the coming Messiah. The New Testament writers read it directly and repeatedly of Jesus, and this study takes that reading seriously and honestly, while naming the older debate rather than hiding it.
basic.originalLanguages: The Servant, ebed, will sprinkle, nazah, many nations, priestly language for cleansing. He carries our avon, iniquity, and His soul becomes an asham, a guilt offering, the technical Levitical sacrifice for sin requiring restitution, here offered by a person rather than an animal. He justifies the rabbim, the many, bearing their sin.
basic.typology: Every detail points forward: the lamb led silently to slaughter, the grave with the rich, the guilt offering made of a life, the intercession for transgressors. The New Testament does not merely allude to this chapter, it treats it as prophecy fulfilled in specific, verifiable detail in the arrest, trial, death, burial, and vindication of Jesus.
basic.christ: Philip explains this very passage to the Ethiopian eunuch and tells him the good news about Jesus, Acts 8:32-35. Peter says Christ suffered for us, bearing our sins in His body, by whose stripes we are healed, quoting verse 5 outright, 1 Peter 2:22-25. Matthew ties Jesus' healing ministry to verse 4, Matthew 8:17. Jesus applies verse 12 to Himself directly the night before He dies, Luke 22:37, He was numbered with the transgressors.
basic.god: God is both the Judge whose justice required that sin be punished, and the Giver who provided, at infinite cost to Himself, the very Servant who bears that punishment. It was the LORD's will to crush Him, a hard truth that this study does not soften, because only in owning it do we see how much God loved us in the doing.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 8:32-35 has Philip preach Jesus straight from this chapter. 1 Peter 2:22-25 quotes it of Christ's sinless, substitutionary suffering. Matthew 8:17 applies verse 4 to Jesus' healing ministry. Luke 22:37 has Jesus quote verse 12 of Himself before His arrest.
basic.oneStory: Every sacrifice, every psalm of the righteous sufferer, every promise that God would deal with sin, converges here: a Servant crushed for the guilt of others, silent before His accusers, buried with the rich, then raised to see His offspring and divide the spoils. This is the center of the Bible's one story, told centuries before it happened, and fulfilled in Jesus Christ.

context.literary: The climactic fourth Servant Song, framed by the LORD's own opening declaration, behold My Servant, and closing verdict, He bore the sin of many; the passage moves from exaltation, through humiliation and death, to vindication, a shape later called the pattern of the cross before the crown.
context.historical: Spoken to exiles who needed to understand not only that God would deliver them from Babylon but how God would finally deal with the sin that caused the exile in the first place; the chapter answers that only a substitute, bearing guilt not his own, can satisfy justice and bring peace.
context.canonical: The single most quoted and alluded-to Old Testament passage in the New Testament's account of the cross, cited or echoed in all four Gospels, Acts, Paul's letters, and 1 Peter, making it the hinge between Israel's sacrificial system and its final, once-for-all fulfillment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal, centuries in advance, that God's promised deliverance would come not through a conquering king first but through a suffering, substitutionary Servant whose death would accomplish what the whole sacrificial system pictured but could never finally achieve.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter is prophetic description of what the Servant would suffer and accomplish; it prescribes nothing directly, but its implicit call, since New Testament times, has always been to believe the report, verse 1, and to receive the healing it describes.

debates.question: Who is the Servant of Isaiah 53?

views.label: Israel corporately
views.case: Some Jewish interpreters, especially from the medieval period onward, read the Servant as the nation of Israel, suffering among and for the nations in exile; earlier Servant passages in Isaiah 41 and 44 do call Israel God's servant.

views.label: A righteous individual, prophet or king
views.case: Others in Jewish tradition have read this of a righteous figure, sometimes identified with Moses, Jeremiah, or an anticipated messianic figure who suffers on Israel's behalf; ancient rabbinic sources, including portions of the Talmud and later midrash, do apply Servant language messianically.

views.label: The Messiah, fulfilled in Jesus of Nazareth
views.case: The New Testament writers, all of them Jewish, read this individually and messianically, and applied it directly to Jesus: His arrest, silent trial, unjust death, burial with a rich man, and resurrection to see His offspring match the chapter point for point, Acts 8:32-35, 1 Peter 2:22-25.

debates.bearing: This study honors the real history of Jewish interpretation, which has never been monolithic, while affirming with the whole New Testament that the Servant is fulfilled precisely and completely in Jesus Christ; the corporate and individual readings are not simply rivals, since Israel's own vocation to suffer for the nations finds its head and fulfillment in the one true Israelite who succeeds where the nation could not.

study.originalLanguages: The ebed, Servant, is sprinkled, nazah, over many nations, priestly cleansing language. He bears avon, iniquity, and His soul becomes an asham, guilt offering, the exact sacrificial term for a sin requiring restitution, here uniquely offered as a life rather than an animal. He justifies the rabbim, the many, language Jesus and Paul both later apply directly to His atoning death.

typology.type: The Servant silent before His shearers, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: Jesus silent before Herod and Pilate, Matthew 27:12-14.

typology.type: A grave with the rich, verse 9
typology.fulfillment: Jesus buried in Joseph of Arimathea's tomb, Matthew 27:57-60.

typology.type: His soul made a guilt offering, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: Christ's death as the once-for-all atoning sacrifice, Hebrews 9:26-28.
Jesus crucified between two criminals, and applying this very verse to Himself, Luke 22:37, Mark 15:27-28.

study.christ: This chapter is fulfilled in Jesus with a precision no other figure in history matches. He grew up in obscurity with no outward majesty; He was despised and rejected; He was silent before His accusers; He was crucified between criminals, numbered with the transgressors, a verse He applies to Himself the night before His death, Luke 22:37; He was buried in a rich man's tomb; and He was raised, seeing His spiritual offspring multiply across the earth. Philip needs no other text to preach Jesus to the Ethiopian eunuch, Acts 8:32-35, and Peter builds his entire theology of the cross on verse 5, by His stripes you were healed, 1 Peter 2:24. This is substitutionary atonement in its clearest Old Testament form: the sinless one bearing the guilt of the many so that they might be justified.
study.god: It was the LORD's will to crush Him, verse 10, a statement that refuses to let us make the cross merely a human injustice; it is that, and it is also the eternal plan of a God who would rather bear the cost of justice Himself than leave His people under it. God is both the one who requires the punishment and the one who, in the Son, absorbs it, the deepest possible answer to how a just God can justify the guilty.

crossReferences.note: Philip preaches Jesus to the Ethiopian eunuch directly from this passage.
Peter quotes verse 5 and 9 of Christ's sinless, substitutionary suffering.
Matthew applies verse 4 to Jesus' healing ministry, He took up our infirmities and bore our diseases.
Jesus quotes verse 12 of Himself the night before His crucifixion, He was numbered with the transgressors.
Paul's by one man's obedience many will be made righteous echoes the Servant justifying the many.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible has been building toward a substitute who can actually bear away sin rather than merely picture its removal. Here that substitute is described centuries in advance: despised, silent, pierced, crushed, buried with the rich, then raised to divide the spoils. The New Testament writers do not read this chapter into Jesus, they read it out of Him, because He is its fulfillment, the Servant who justifies the many by bearing what was theirs to bear.

sermon.title: The Sin-Bearer
sermon.why: Preached by Timothy Keller at Redeemer Presbyterian Church on February 21, 2016 directly from Isaiah 52:13-53:12, this sermon walks through the ordinariness, the violence, and the vicariousness of the Servant's suffering, showing how the passage subverts every human category of salvation and points to the substitutionary work of Christ.

quiz.q: What does it mean that the Servant's soul is made an asham, a guilt offering, in verse 10?

options.text: His death functions as the specific Levitical sacrifice for guilt requiring restitution, offered as a life rather than an animal.
options.note: Asham is the exact technical term for the guilt offering, uniquely applied here to a person.

options.text: It means His suffering was simply an unfortunate accident.
options.note: The text says it was the LORD's will, and names it with precise sacrificial vocabulary.

options.text: It refers to a burnt offering of grain, unrelated to sin.
options.note: Asham specifically denotes the guilt offering, not a grain or burnt offering.

options.text: It shows God punishing an innocent bystander at random.
options.note: It rightly senses that the Servant is innocent, but the offering language shows deliberate, purposeful substitution, not randomness.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament apply Isaiah 53 to Jesus?

options.text: Directly and repeatedly, including Philip's preaching to the Ethiopian eunuch, Peter's teaching on the cross, and Jesus' own quotation of verse 12 before His death.
options.note: Acts 8, 1 Peter 2, and Luke 22 all apply this chapter to Jesus explicitly.

options.text: It is never mentioned in the New Testament.
options.note: It is one of the most quoted Old Testament chapters in the New Testament.

options.text: Only symbolically, with no specific verses quoted.
options.note: Specific verses are quoted directly, not merely alluded to symbolically.

options.text: Only by later church tradition, never by New Testament writers themselves.
options.note: Later tradition does draw on this chapter extensively, but the New Testament writers themselves, Luke, Peter, Matthew, apply it directly and first.

quiz.q: Historically, how have Jewish interpreters read the identity of the Servant?

options.text: Variously, some reading Israel corporately, others a righteous individual or messianic figure, showing the interpretive question is real and has never been monolithic.
options.note: Honest engagement recognizes both corporate and individual Jewish readings existed.

options.text: All Jewish interpreters throughout history have read it identically as Israel and nothing else.
options.note: The corporate Israel reading is prominent and real, but this overstates uniformity; some strands, including rabbinic sources, read it messianically.

options.text: No Jewish source before Christian times ever discussed this chapter.
options.note: The chapter was read and discussed well before and alongside early Christian interpretation.

options.text: Jewish tradition unanimously identified the Servant as Jesus of Nazareth before the New Testament was written.
options.note: This is inaccurate; the messianic and Christian reading develops distinctly, especially with the New Testament writers.

quiz.q: What is the effect of the phrase 'the punishment that brought us peace was upon Him' in verse 5?

options.text: It teaches substitution: the Servant bears the punishment so that those who deserved it receive peace instead.
options.note: This is the heart of substitutionary atonement expressed in the verse.

options.text: It teaches that punishment and peace are unrelated ideas placed side by side.
options.note: The verse deliberately links the two as cause and effect.

options.text: It means the Servant made peace by avoiding all punishment.
options.note: This inverts the verse; He does not avoid punishment, He receives it fully so others gain peace.

options.text: It suggests peace comes only from human effort to be good.
options.note: The peace described flows from the Servant's suffering, not human effort.

quiz.q: What happens to the Servant after His suffering, according to verses 10-12?

options.text: He sees His offspring, prolongs His days, is satisfied, and divides the spoils with the strong, a resurrection and vindication.
options.note: These verses describe life and triumph following the Servant's death.

options.text: He simply ceases to exist with no further mention.
options.note: The text explicitly describes His continued life and exaltation afterward.

options.text: He is forgotten by those He died for.
options.note: The opposite; He justifies many and they are counted among His offspring.

options.text: He returns to obscurity with no lasting effect.
options.note: The opposite; He is allotted a portion with the great and divides the spoils, language of triumph, not obscurity.

questions: Verse 3 says we esteemed Him not, that people hid their faces from Him. Where are you tempted to be embarrassed by, or quietly hide, the suffering, crucified Christ rather than glory in Him?

Verse 6 says we all like sheep have gone astray, each one turning to his own way. What is your own way that you have followed instead of God's, and how does it feel to know your specific iniquity was laid on Him?
The Servant was silent before His accusers, verse 7, though innocent. Where is God calling you to trust Him rather than defend or vindicate yourself?
By His stripes we are healed, verse 5. What wound, physical, emotional, or spiritual, do you need to bring honestly to the healing this verse promises?
The Servant made intercession for the transgressors, verse 12, even as He was numbered among them. Who is someone difficult in your life you are being called to intercede for rather than condemn?

translationNotes.note: Where BSB prints em dashes bounding the clause His appearance was disfigured beyond that of any man, and His form was marred beyond human likeness, they have been rendered as commas per house style.

Guilt offering renders asham, the specific Levitical sacrifice of Leviticus 5-7; its application here to a person rather than an animal is unique in the Old Testament and central to reading this chapter as substitutionary atonement.
By His knowledge can also be read by knowledge of Him, that is, those who know Him are justified; both readings keep the emphasis on the Servant as the ground of justification.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 52:13-53:12. One em dash pair rendered as commas per house style, noted in translationNotes. Sermon verified this session at podcast.gospelinlife.com.
